summary: Big moves happening in the SE BeltLine corridor today with Reynoldstown finally seeing construction start on that long-delayed Chester Avenue townhome project, plus a new Asian-American restaurant opening in Inman Park and Lewis Barbecue bringing Texas-style brisket to Ansley Mall. Meanwhile, the whole city's dealing with airport chaos as TSA agents work without pay.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near BeltLine Finally Breaks Ground After 5 Years
topStory.summary: That empty lot where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development has started construction on 85 townhomes and condos just a block from the BeltLine's Eastside Trail, five years after the original buildings were torn down. The project had been stuck in permitting limbo since Toll Brothers cleared the site.
topStory.body:
If you've been wondering what was happening with that cleared corner lot near the BeltLine in Reynoldstown, wonder no more. After sitting empty for nearly five years, the 2-acre site where Chester Avenue meets Fulton Terrace is finally seeing construction crews break ground on what will become 85 townhomes and condos.

Embry Development took over the project from national builder Toll Brothers two years ago, inheriting the permits for what they're now calling a mixed residential development. The location couldn't be better for BeltLine lovers, sitting just a block west of the Eastside Trail and about a block north of Memorial Drive. It's the kind of infill development the corridor has been waiting for.

The timing feels right too, with Reynoldstown continuing its transformation from overlooked neighborhood to one of the hottest spots along the BeltLine. Between the trail access, proximity to Grant Park, and all the new restaurants and shops popping up along Memorial, this corner was just begging for something to happen. Now residents will finally get to see what all that cleared space was waiting for.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site showing early groundwork at the Chester Avenue development in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 18 'Missing Middle' Houses for Under $700k
stories.summary: Finally, some relatively affordable housing news in Decatur. Cottages at Midway is launching next week with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards on Midway Road. With Decatur's median home values hitting $700k, this village-style community is trying to fill the gap for people who want to live ITP without breaking the bank.
stories.body:
Anyone who's looked at houses in Decatur lately knows the sticker shock is real, with median home values climbing past $700,000. That's why Cottages at Midway feels like such a breath of fresh air. This new community on Midway Road is launching next week with 18 standalone houses designed around the 'missing middle' concept — more than a townhouse, less than a mansion.

The development sits on 2.1 acres of what used to be underused lots, now transformed into a pocket neighborhood with shared courtyards and green space. It's the kind of thoughtful infill development that Decatur has been talking about but rarely delivers. The houses are built around community spaces rather than just cramming as many units as possible onto the land.

The timing couldn't be better, with so many people priced out of traditional Decatur neighborhoods but still wanting to live close to downtown and MARTA. If this model works — and the early interest suggests it will — expect to see more of these village-style developments pop up around the area. It's exactly the kind of creative housing solution ITP needs right now.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing the layout of Cottages at Midway village-style housing development

stories.headline: West End Townhomes Along BeltLine Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: Those West End townhomes near the BeltLine are finally rising up along Hopkins Street. Ackerman & Co. (the same folks behind Lee + White) started construction last year on 23 for-sale townhomes, now renamed GW Parkside. The first seven units should hit the market this spring, bringing some much-needed for-sale housing to this BeltLine stretch.
stories.body:
Walking along the BeltLine near the West End, you can finally see some vertical progress at the GW Parkside development. Ackerman & Co., the same developer behind the popular Lee + White complex, has been working on this 23-unit townhome project for about a year now, and the first phase is starting to look like actual homes.

What makes this project interesting is how rare for-sale townhomes are along this stretch of the BeltLine. Most of the development has been rental apartments or single-family renovations, so having new construction townhomes for purchase fills a real gap in the market. The location is pretty sweet too, walking distance to Lee + White's restaurants and shops, plus easy BeltLine access.

The developer says the first seven homes should be ready this spring, with marketing materials already going up on site. Given how hot the West End has gotten and how few new construction options there are for buyers along the BeltLine, these are likely to move quickly once they hit the market.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction near the BeltLine in West End

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Opens in Inman Park with Soft Shell Crab Bao
stories.summary: A new Asian-American comfort food spot just opened on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park. Mushi Ni, from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, is serving everything from soft shell crab bao to Peking chicken platters right off the Freedom Park Trail. It's their first restaurant after years of pop-ups around town.
stories.body:
The pop-up scene in Atlanta just got a little less crowded because Mushi Ni finally has a permanent home. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first brick-and-mortar restaurant today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, right where you'd want it — just steps from the Freedom Park Trail.

The menu reads like everything you'd want from an Asian-American comfort food kitchen. Think soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, crab Rangoon puffs, and their signature Peking chicken platter that feeds two for $25. They're also doing Thai tea martinis and other creative cocktails, because this is Inman Park and people expect their drinks to be interesting.

Both chefs bring serious credentials — Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, while Le has been grinding in local kitchens for years. After building a following with their pop-ups, it feels like the right time and place for them to plant roots. The location is perfect for both neighborhood regulars and BeltLine walkers looking for something different.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin Brisket to the BeltLine
stories.summary: The pitmaster behind some of Austin's best barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall, complete with panoramic views of the new BeltLine bridge. Lewis Barbecue has six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a glass-walled loft space, turning out what might be Atlanta's best brisket. The location behind Cook's Warehouse couldn't be better for BeltLine walkers.
stories.body:
John Lewis didn't just bring his barbecue to Atlanta — he brought his whole operation, smokers and all. The new Lewis Barbecue at Ansley Mall features something I've never seen: a glass-walled smokehouse loft with panoramic views of the BeltLine bridge. Six massive 22-foot smokers run around the clock in staggered eight-hour shifts, making it look like the fanciest engine room in Georgia.

Lewis, who helped weld those smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, is turning out some serious brisket. The early reviews suggest this might be the best barbecue to hit Atlanta in years, with that perfect balance of smoke and heat that made him famous in Austin and Charleston. The setup is pure theater — you can watch the whole operation through those big windows.

The location is perfect timing too, with the new pedestrian bridge connecting Ansley Mall directly to the BeltLine. After a long walk on the trail, there's something deeply satisfying about ending up at a place where you can smell the smoke from blocks away. It's exactly the kind of destination the BeltLine has been missing.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Food Hall Opens Mid-April Near East Lake
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food hall concept to the eastside. Vela Park Food Works, located in the new rental community near East Lake Golf Club, will feature six chef-driven concepts when it opens mid-April. It's exactly the kind of food destination the eastside has been waiting for.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to build a food hall. The founder of New York City's legendary Gansevoort Market has already proven himself in Atlanta with successful concepts, and now he's bringing his expertise to the eastside with Vela Park Food Works.

The new food hall sits at the center of the nearly 12-acre Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, featuring six different chef-driven concepts alongside craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. The timing is perfect — mid-April opening — just as spring hits and people want to eat outside again.

What makes this particularly exciting is the location. The eastside has been underserved when it comes to serious food destinations, especially compared to the BeltLine corridor further west. Having someone with Montwaid's track record bring his vision to this part of town could be a real game-changer for the area. Plus, the combination of food hall and residential community creates the kind of live-work-eat environment that Atlanta does so well.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing dining areas and kitchen spaces

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Project Nearly Done
stories.summary: That Complete Streets makeover of West Howard Avenue in Decatur is almost finished after nearly a decade in planning. The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle now features a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures. It's a major transformation from the old drag strip it used to be.
stories.body:
Anyone who used to drive West Howard Avenue in Decatur remembers it as a bit of a speedway — the kind of road where cars flew through without much thought for pedestrians or cyclists. That's all changing now that the Complete Streets project is wrapping up after nearly 10 years of planning and design.

The transformation is pretty dramatic. The roughly one-mile stretch from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) all the way to Paden Circle at East Lake MARTA now features a protected two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, brand new sidewalks, and infrastructure designed to slow down traffic. It's the kind of thoughtful redesign that actually makes walking and biking feel safe.

What's remarkable is how long this took — nearly a decade from concept to completion. But seeing the finished product, it's clear why it was worth the wait. This isn't just slapping some paint on the road and calling it bike infrastructure. It's a complete rethinking of how a street can work for everyone, not just cars. Other cities in the metro should be taking notes.
stories.imageAlt: New two-way cycle track with concrete barriers along West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: Atlantic Station Tops National 'Best Places to Live' List
stories.summary: Atlantic Station just claimed the #1 spot on Niche.com's 'Best Places to Live in America' list, with Midtown coming in at #6. Out of over 18,000 neighborhoods ranked nationwide, Atlanta snagged two spots in the top 10. The rankings considered everything from public schools to local amenities and job opportunities.
stories.body:
Well, this is a nice surprise. Atlantic Station just got named the best place to live in America by Niche.com, beating out over 18,000 other neighborhoods across the country. And if that wasn't enough good news for Atlanta, Midtown landed at #6 on the same list, giving us 20% of the top 10 spots.

The rankings looked at all the stuff that actually matters when you're deciding where to live: public schools, cost of living, job opportunities, crime rates, and local amenities. Data came from the FBI, U.S. Census, and CDC, so this isn't just some feel-good chamber of commerce marketing. These neighborhoods genuinely scored well across multiple categories.

What's interesting is how both Atlantic Station and Midtown represent different flavors of urban living done right. Atlantic Station gives you that master-planned community feel with everything walkable, while Midtown offers the classic urban neighborhood experience with great transit access. Both have managed to create the kind of live-work-play environment that people actually want, which apparently the rest of the country is noticing.
stories.imageAlt: Aerial view of Atlantic Station showing mixed-use development and walkable streets

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Controls as Fire Union Fight Heats Up
stories.summary: The Atlanta City Council's Public Safety Committee moved forward two resolutions aimed at controlling ICE activities in the city, while dozens of residents showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' union contract. The ICE measures would ban detention facilities and require documentation when agents work with Atlanta police.
stories.body:
The Atlanta City Council took aim at federal immigration enforcement Monday, unanimously advancing two resolutions through the Public Safety Committee that would significantly limit ICE activities within city limits. One resolution would ban ICE detention facilities entirely, while the other requires Atlanta police to document whenever ICE agents are present during their operations.

But ICE wasn't the only hot topic. Dozens of residents packed the meeting to blast Mayor Andre Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever firefighters' union contract, which the council actually ratified back in April 2025. The disconnect between what the council voted for and what the mayor is willing to sign has created a messy political situation that's clearly frustrating residents.

Both ICE resolutions head to the full council for a vote on April 20. Given the unanimous committee support and the current political climate around immigration enforcement, they'll likely pass. The firefighter contract situation, however, remains stuck in limbo between a council that supports it and a mayor who won't sign it.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Found on Atlanta BeltLine Sparks Safety Warning
stories.summary: Someone spotted an 8-to-10-foot python on the BeltLine yesterday, and video shows a man handling the massive snake with his bare hands. Wildlife experts are warning people not to touch any snakes they find, especially ones that clearly aren't native to Georgia. No word on whether this was someone's escaped pet or what.
stories.body:
Just when you think you've seen everything on the BeltLine, someone finds an 8-foot python. Joseph Ragland captured video of the incident, showing what appears to be a Burmese python being pulled from bushes along the trail by a man who clearly wasn't fazed by handling a massive constrictor snake.

'That's not a typical Georgia snake; that's something different,' Ragland said, which is probably the understatement of the year. The video shows about 10 feet of snake laid out on the BeltLine path, with no clear indication of whether the man handling it was the owner or just someone who really likes reptiles.

Jason Clark from Southeastern Reptile Rescue called the video 'very concerning' and warned people against handling any wildlife they encounter on the trail. Whether this was an escaped pet or something else entirely, it's a good reminder that you never know what you'll find on Atlanta's most popular walking path. Maybe stick to worrying about cyclists and scooters.
